title: Popular raw dog, cat food sold in Illinois, other states recalled for salmonella, listeria
summary: Pet food safety officials are warning that certain raw dog and cat foods have been recalled due to possible Salmonella and Listeria contamination. The recall affects products sold in Illinois and multiple other states, putting both dogs and cats at risk. Salmonella and Listeria can cause vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, fever, and loss of appetite in pets, and infected animals can transmit the bacteria to people through handling, feeding, or contact with contaminated surfaces. The incident highlights ongoing concerns about raw pet diets and the risk of cross-contamination in homes. Pet owners should stop feeding the recalled food immediately, discard it safely, wash hands and bowls, and contact a veterinarian if their pet shows signs of illness.

title: Acetaminophen rises to top 10 pet poisons in 2025
summary: Pet Poison Helpline released its 2025 Annual Report Card and Top 10 Pet Poisons list in January 2026, summarizing a year of veterinary toxicology guidance across the United States. The report highlights that acetaminophen, a common over-the-counter human pain reliever, rose into the top 10 most common pet poisons for the year. The findings apply to dogs, cats, and other companion animals, underscoring that everyday medications can be dangerous when pets access them. The ranking is intended to help pet owners and veterinary professionals identify emerging poisoning trends and prioritize prevention. Because even small doses of some human drugs can be toxic, experts stress secure storage, careful administration, and immediate veterinary consultation if ingestion is suspected. The report serves as a reminder to treat household medicines and other toxins as potential hazards and keep a poison helpline or veterinary contact readily available.

title: Daffodils can be 'dangerously toxic' to dogs and cats pet owners warned
summary: During February 2026, pet owners faced a series of warnings and incidents involving toxic exposures in dogs and cats. Veterinary outlets urged dog owners not to delay care after contact with a lesser-known toxin, while Vet Times and the Derry Journal identified rhododendron, azalea, and daffodils as dangerously toxic to companion animals. In Lincoln, Nebraska, police launched an investigation after a dog was poisoned during a burglary, with local news reporting the case alongside a related break-in. These stories underscore that pet poisoning can stem from common garden plants, environmental toxins, or intentional harm. Recognizing symptoms quickly and seeking immediate veterinary care can save lives. Owners should also secure their homes and yards, monitor pets outdoors, and remove or restrict access to known toxic plants. Affected animals include dogs and cats.

sources.name: KOLN | Nebraska Local News, Weather, Sports | Lincoln, NE

title: Xylitol, a commonly used compound in gum and medications, poses danger to pets
summary: On February 26, 2026, MSN and CBS News published reports warning pet owners about the dangers of xylitol, a sugar substitute common in sugar-free gum and some medications. The coverage highlights that xylitol can be toxic to pets, including cats and other animals, and serves as a reminder that everyday household products can pose serious poisoning risks. Ingesting xylitol can trigger a rapid insulin release, leading to dangerous hypoglycemia, seizures, liver failure, and even death if not treated promptly. Because many items—such as gum, candy, toothpaste, baked goods, and certain drugs—may contain xylitol, owners should read labels carefully and keep such products out of reach. The reports emphasize the importance of recognizing symptoms quickly and seeking immediate veterinary care. As xylitol becomes more common in foods and medicines, awareness and prevention are key to protecting pets.

title: RCL Foods recalls pet food over Salmonella contamination risk
summary: Pet food and treat products have been recalled in recent weeks because of bacterial contamination concerns. Food Poison Journal reported on March 1 that dog treats were recalled due to Salmonella, while PetfoodIndustry reported on March 6 that RCL Foods recalled pet food over Salmonella contamination risk. PIX11 reported on March 20 that a pet food recall involved Salmonella, Listeria, and E. coli. The affected animals are primarily dogs and may include other pets. Salmonella can cause vomiting, diarrhea, fever, and lethargy in animals and can be transmitted to humans through handling contaminated food or contact with infected pets. Listeria and E. coli add additional serious health risks. Pet owners should check product labels, stop feeding recalled items, and contact a veterinarian if symptoms appear. The recalls highlight the importance of monitoring pet food safety and practicing proper food handling to protect both pets and people.

title: Wide range of dog and cat food in Japan found to contain toxic PFAS: research
summary: Recent coverage across three continents highlights growing concerns about toxic substances in pet food and treats. In Japan, a research investigation found a wide range of dog and cat food containing PFAS, chemicals linked to long-term health risks. A U.S. consumer guide examined how to choose natural, organic and non-toxic dog food amid rising worries about chemical contamination. In Ireland, veterinarians warned dog and cat owners that common Easter foods such as chocolate, raisins and xylitol sweets can be poisonous. Together these reports show that both industrial contaminants and everyday seasonal foods can endanger dogs and cats. The findings matter because pets depend on processed foods and treats, and exposure to PFAS or accidental ingestion of toxic human foods can cause acute illness or chronic disease. Owners should monitor labels, avoid known toxins, and seek veterinary help if poisoning is suspected.

title: Hidden Killers in Your Home: Common Toxins Poisoning Pets Every Day - A-Z Animals
summary: Pet safety experts are warning that everyday household items and seasonal Easter decorations can become deadly toxins for dogs and cats. A-Z Animals highlights common hidden killers found in homes, while the Pet Poison Helpline cautions that Easter baskets, lilies, chocolate, and plastic grass pose special holiday risks. Pets that chew, lick, or swallow these substances can develop vomiting, seizures, kidney failure, or life-threatening blockages. The reports matter because many toxins are items owners already have on hand—human foods, medications, plants, and small decorative objects—and exposure can rapidly turn into an emergency. With spring holidays arriving, veterinarians urge owners to pet-proof baskets, secure trash and medications, verify food safety, and keep a poison-hotline number accessible. Early prevention and fast action are the best ways to protect pets.

title: These spring flowers may be toxic to cats and dogs — pet parents beware
summary: As spring arrives, multiple pet-safety reports are warning owners about common household and seasonal hazards for cats and dogs. Recent coverage highlights two key risks: everyday foods that are toxic to cats and spring flowers that can poison both cats and dogs. Catster published a vet-reviewed guide identifying 16 common foods dangerous to cats, while Yahoo Lifestyle Canada, The Spruce Pets, and Daily Paws have flagged tulips and other seasonal blooms as toxic to pets. The reports note that cats are particularly vulnerable, with some flowers causing severe illness if chewed or ingested. Veterinarians and pet experts emphasize awareness, prevention, and keeping hazardous items out of reach. With spring gardening and seasonal bouquets bringing more plants into homes, these warnings matter for pet owners wanting to avoid emergency vet visits and keep cats and dogs safe.

title: Raw dog food recall grows to more than 180 lots amid contamination concerns and reported illnesses
summary: A wave of pet-safety alerts in late May 2026 has prompted recalls and warnings for dog and cat owners. Albright’s Raw Pet Food voluntarily recalled select raw chicken dog food recipes over potential Salmonella contamination, with products sold nationwide and in multiple states. AllProvide recalled dog food in at least nine states, including New York, due to possible plastic contamination. Raaw Energy expanded a raw dog food recall for Listeria monocytogenes; the recall now covers dozens of products and more than 180 lots, with reported illnesses in dogs and possible risk to humans. Separately, veterinary outlets warned that lilies, Alstroemeria and other common plants are toxic—especially to cats—and that essential oils can be unsafe for pets. These incidents highlight contamination risks in raw pet food and household toxins, making it important for owners to check FDA recall notices, remove affected products, and seek veterinary guidance for symptoms or toxin exposure.

title: 30 Common Houseplants Toxic to Pets (A to Z): Cat, Dog, and Bird Safety Guide
summary: Recent coverage highlights the danger that common garden and houseplants pose to pets, especially cats. A USA Today article warns gardeners to skip plants that are toxic to cats, while Encyclopedia Britannica explains why lilies are particularly deadly to felines and can cause rapid kidney failure from even small exposures. Meanwhile, Farmers' Almanac has published an A-to-Z guide of 30 common houseplants that can harm cats, dogs, and birds. These plants are widely found in homes, gardens, and floral arrangements, and pet owners may not recognize warning signs such as vomiting, drooling, lethargy, or organ failure until serious illness develops. The reports matter because they offer practical, timely guidance to prevent accidental poisoning, reduce emergency vet visits, and help owners choose safer greenery.

title: Recalled Puppy Formula Neglecting Sickened Shelter Pups
summary: A report from Truth about Pet Food, syndicated through Google News, is warning pet owners about a recalled puppy formula that has been linked to sick shelter puppies. The incident highlights a breakdown in care for vulnerable dogs—specifically young puppies in a shelter environment—after they were fed a formula that was later recalled. While the exact brand, batch numbers, and number of affected animals remain unclear from the available reports, the affected species is dogs. The case underscores the importance of monitoring pet food recalls, especially for vulnerable populations like shelter puppies that depend on caregivers to provide safe nutrition. Pet owners and shelter staff should check current recall alerts, stop feeding any recalled formula immediately, and consult a veterinarian if puppies show signs of vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, or poor appetite. Because shelters often lack resources for rapid veterinary intervention, quick action and public awareness can help prevent further illness.

title: Pet owners in 20 states need to know about this expanded food recall that poses serious neurological risks
summary: Go Raw has expanded a recall of certain freeze-dried dog and cat food products, primarily under the Steve’s Real Food label, because they may contain low levels of vitamin B1 (thiamine). The recall, first reported in early June 2026, affects roughly 20 U.S. states. Thiamine deficiency can cause serious neurological symptoms in pets—such as weakness, tremors, unsteady gait, seizures, and lethargy. The FDA and multiple outlets, including KVUE, KLTV, USA Today, and The Independent, have flagged the nationwide distribution. The company is urging consumers to stop feeding the affected products immediately, discard or return them, and watch pets for signs of illness. Because the freeze-dried foods were sold widely, the recall carries a meaningful risk of vitamin-deficiency-related nerve injury if the recalled food is not removed from pets' diets.

title: Pet Poison Helpline Named a Minnesota Star Tribune Top Workplace for the 16th Time
summary: Pet Poison Helpline has been named a Minnesota Star Tribune Top Workplace for the 16th time, the organization announced on June 15, 2026. The recognition highlights the helpline's workplace culture and its continued role as a 24/7 resource for pet owners and veterinary professionals. When callers contact Pet Poison Helpline, they reach a real person trained to assess potential poison exposures in companion animals, including dogs, cats, and other pets. The award underscores the importance of reliable, accessible veterinary toxicology guidance during emergencies involving medications, foods, plants, and household chemicals. For pet owners, this means a trusted service remains staffed and available to help evaluate risks and guide next steps when animals may have ingested something dangerous. The Top Workplace distinction is based on employee feedback, suggesting the organization maintains strong support for the staff who handle urgent pet-safety calls. Affected animals: other.

title: Voluntary Recall of Two Lots of PEDIGREE® Can High Protein Chopped Chicken & Duck Flavor Wet Dog Food Due to Potential Fraudulent Distribution of Product Which May Contain Foreign Material
summary: Mars Petcare has issued a voluntary recall of two lots of PEDIGREE High Protein Chopped Chicken & Duck Flavor wet dog food after reports that the product may contain foreign material, including sharp metal and plastic. The FDA and multiple national and local news outlets announced the recall on July 2–3, 2026, noting the affected cans were distributed nationwide. The contamination is believed to be linked to potential fraudulent distribution of the product. While no widespread injuries have been detailed in the coverage, the recall is precautionary. Dog owners who purchased the affected lots are urged to stop feeding the food immediately, check lot codes and expiration dates, and contact the retailer or Mars Petcare for a refund or replacement. The incident highlights ongoing concerns about foreign material contamination in pet food and the importance of rapid recall communication to protect pets.
